L1.area: Asynchronous stream processing and data framing infrastructure

L2.description: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in an asynchronous I/O framing component due to an unsound safety assertion that allows a pinned wrapper type to be moved freely. The inner I/O stream, which may depend on stable memory placement, can be invalidated when the enclosing type is relocated after pinning. This violates Rust's pin safety guarantees and can lead to memory corruption, arbitrary code execution, or denial of service via a crafted I/O type.

L3.area: The codec crate's I/O framing layer that unifies stream and sink interfaces for encoding and decoding
L3.description: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in the codec crate's asynchronous I/O framing component due to an unsound blanket trait implementation that unconditionally marks the wrapper type as safe to relocate in memory. When the inner I/O stream relies on stable memory placement (i.e., it is not inherently movable after pinning), the wrapper's unsafe pin projections hand out pinned references to memory that may have already been moved. This violates Rust's pin safety guarantees and can lead to memory corruption, arbitrary code execution, or denial of service.

ground_truth.reason: The Framed struct has an unsound blanket Unpin implementation, allowing it to be moved after pinning while its methods use unsafe Pin::new_unchecked on the inner I/O stream, leading to use-after-free when the inner type relies on pin guarantees.
